fixtures.summary: High-mobility traveler who wants overland movement and scenic rail to be a core part of the trip rather than dead time.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I can handle frequent moves if the route itself is part of the experience. I would rather cross a region by rail than fly over it.
planning_style_notes: comfortable booking independent rail segments
wants a route with narrative flow

budget_model.splurge_style: pay for scenic rail or ferry legs that materially improve the route

mode_anchors.label: Long-form rail segments
mode_anchors.notes: Route should privilege trains over flights when feasible.

regional_adjacency_preferences.label: Contiguous overland progression
regional_adjacency_preferences.notes: Avoid backtracking unless a major payoff exists.

route_modes.notes: Rail and ferry are part of the identity of the trip.

direct_statements: Traveler says transit is part of the trip.
revealed_preferences: Traveler picks overland bundles over direct flights.

confidence_notes: High signal because both stated and option-choice evidence align.

evidence.note: Traveler wants the route itself to be a major part of the trip.
Selected a coherent rail chain instead of a cheaper disconnected flight package.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: This traveler is not merely tolerant of movement; they actively want scenic transit and coherent overland flow.
expected_tensions: May accept more moves than average, but still benefits from route logic that avoids wasteful backtracking.
planning_implications: Offer route bundles where the transit segments are a feature rather than a penalty.
Use flights only when they protect a materially better overland structure.

fixtures.summary: Depth-oriented city traveler who values historic layers, museums, and urban walking more than broad destination coverage.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I would rather spend five dense days in one great city than race through four capitals. I care about museums, neighborhoods, and historic texture.
planning_style_notes: comfortable with transit-rich cities
prefers a researched daily structure

experience_anchors.label: Museum and old-city days
experience_anchors.notes: Historic urban immersion matters more than countryside exposure.

evidence.note: Traveler explicitly rejects fast multi-city hopping in favor of depth.
Traveler reacts positively to a museum district day with preselected targets.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: Depth and cultural-historic attention dominate this profile, so route sprawl should be penalized early.
expected_tensions: Iconic sites matter, but they are acceptable mainly when they fit a deeper urban immersion plan.
planning_implications: Use fewer bases with denser local cultural menus.
Prefer neighborhood and museum clustering over broader regional coverage.

fixtures.summary: Traveler with moderate overall budget discipline who will pay for standout meals and market experiences if the value is obvious.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I do not need luxury hotels, but I do want the option to spend real money on meals that feel special or locally important.
planning_style_notes: wants side-by-side options to judge value
likes mixing splurges with simpler days

budget_model.splurge_style: selectively splurge on destination-defining meals and cooking experiences

food.notes: Food is a planning driver rather than just a supporting factor.

experience_anchors.label: Destination-defining meals
experience_anchors.notes: Not every dinner, but some meals should be major events.

evidence.note: Traveler frames food as a major reason for choosing destinations.
Traveler chooses shorter transit and higher meal budget over a scenic detour.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: Food is one of the few elements that can override otherwise moderate budget discipline.
expected_tensions: The planner should protect food-value opportunities without silently upgrading lodging or transit across the board.
planning_implications: Present 2-3 concrete restaurant and food-experience options to calibrate revealed value preference.
Protect splurge budget for meals while keeping other categories disciplined.

fixtures.summary: Traveler who values exploratory wandering, contemporary life, and locally textured discovery over heavily curated landmark lists.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I like neighborhoods where I can walk with only a loose direction and keep stumbling into places that look interesting.
planning_style_notes: does not want every day tightly scripted
still benefits from strong wandering zones

experience_anchors.label: Strong wandering districts
experience_anchors.notes: The planner should suggest places to wander toward rather than only fixed bookings.

evidence.note: Traveler asks for neighborhoods and streets, not a checklist of sights.
Traveler likes lively streets but still wants solo movement freedom.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: This profile needs strong discovery scaffolding without heavy over-structuring.
expected_tensions: The traveler still needs some directional guidance so wandering does not become aimless or low-value.
planning_implications: Surface wandering zones, neighborhoods, and loose thematic routes.
Avoid turning every day into a reservation-driven plan.

fixtures.summary: Traveler who enjoys lively atmospheres and social contact but burns out if every day is packed or every night is late.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I like cafes, bars, street life, and a sense that things are happening, but I hit a wall if the trip is intense every day.
planning_style_notes: wants recovery blocks built into the trip
likes a social environment more than a nonstop schedule

avoid: stacking demanding nights back to back

tension_flags.description: Traveler wants lively environments but needs rhythm-protective recovery blocks.

evidence.note: Traveler wants street energy but explicitly describes burnout risk.
Traveler asks to remove one packed evening after seeing the draft.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: The planner should not misread social appetite as intensity tolerance.
expected_tensions: High-social-energy days need explicit recovery design, not just optimistic pacing.
planning_implications: Protect easy mornings, flexible afternoons, or lighter transfer days after big social blocks.
Prefer places with ambient social life over plans that require constant high-energy participation.

fixtures.summary: Traveler with a meaningful comfort floor who still wants real exploration, but not at the cost of sleep, safety, or booking simplicity.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I do not need luxury for its own sake, but I do need a quiet, clean room, easy arrivals, and logistics that do not feel chaotic.
planning_style_notes: willing to pay for smoother logistics
does not want heroic transfers

quality_floors.lodging: quiet private room in a central area

budget_model.splurge_style: spend to avoid disruption and poor sleep

quality_floor_anchors.label: Quiet central lodging
quality_floor_anchors.notes: A poor room can drag down the whole trip.

evidence.note: Traveler sets a hard lodging quality floor.
Traveler chooses a smoother but slightly more expensive transfer package.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: This traveler needs the planner to respect comfort floors as real constraints rather than optional upgrades.
expected_tensions: Budget discipline still matters, but not enough to justify noisy or fragile logistics.
planning_implications: Carry quality-floor checks into option filtering before ranking.
Offer a narrower but cleaner option set rather than a long list of false economy choices.

fixtures.summary: Traveler who strongly prefers route logic and geographic coherence over eclectic contrast, even when multiple regions look attractive on paper.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I dislike trips that bounce around just because individual cities are interesting. I want the route to make sense on a map.
planning_style_notes: cares about map logic
happy to skip good places that break the route

evidence.note: Traveler names geographic coherence as a trip-defining rule.
Traveler explicitly cares about geographic logic.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: This profile should bias toward elegant routes with fewer discontinuities, even if the candidate list becomes narrower.
expected_tensions: May give up individually attractive destinations to preserve route quality.
planning_implications: Score route shape early instead of only ranking destinations independently.
Use adjacency and backtracking penalties aggressively.

fixtures.summary: Traveler wants to see a lot over a limited window but also knows they fatigue quickly when transfers and busy days pile up.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I want to cover a lot because I may not be back soon, but I also do badly when every few days are packed and I need time to reset.
planning_style_notes: anxious about missing out
wants pacing help

tension_flags.description: Traveler wants coverage that exceeds their sustainable pace without route discipline.

evidence.note: Traveler describes strong FOMO alongside a need for reset time.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: The engine should not average these values into a mushy middle; it should surface a genuine pacing tension.
expected_tensions: Breadth desires are real, but route clustering and recovery blocks are mandatory.
planning_implications: Offer clustered variants that trade breadth against move density transparently.
Make the opportunity cost of extra stops explicit.

fixtures.summary: Traveler wants open-ended discovery but has enough fixed places and calendar anchors that the trip cannot simply drift.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I want the trip to feel open and exploratory, but I also know there are a few places and events I really do not want to miss.
planning_style_notes: likes flexibility
still committed to several specific stops

place_anchors.label: Cappadocia
place_anchors.notes: Important anchor but timing is somewhat flexible.

tension_flags.description: Traveler wants flexible discovery inside a trip that has several immovable anchor commitments.

evidence.note: Traveler names specific cities and a fixed event date.
Traveler still wants much of the trip to feel unstructured and exploratory.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: The planner should preserve elasticity in the spaces between anchors, not pretend the trip can stay fully open-ended.
expected_tensions: Discovery remains important, but anchor commitments should shape the route skeleton first.
planning_implications: Build around fixed anchors, then allocate elastic days and wandering zones around them.
Do not oversell full spontaneity where calendar commitments make it impossible.

fixtures.summary: Cost-disciplined traveler who optimizes every category for value and accepts friction, logistics self-reliance, and lower comfort floors in exchange for budget efficiency.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I want to see as much as possible without spending more than I need to. I am comfortable booking my own trains, staying in simple places, and doing research myself.
planning_style_notes: wants cheapest viable option not just average option
happy to trade convenience for savings

direct_statements: Traveler explicitly references a hard budget ceiling and comfort with doing logistics themselves.
revealed_preferences: Traveler selects cheaper self-managed options over packaged alternatives.

confidence_notes: High signal because both stated constraint and option-choice evidence align on cost discipline.

evidence.note: Traveler names budget ceiling and comfort with self-managed logistics.
Traveler selects independent public transport over guided package at roughly half the cost.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: This traveler treats budget discipline as a first-order constraint rather than a secondary preference. Value efficiency should guide every category, not just lodging.
expected_tensions: Budget ceiling may force route contraction or longer transit legs that reduce overall experience quality.
planning_implications: Present cheapest viable option prominently, not just middle-of-range options.
Do not silently upgrade any category without surfacing the cost delta.
Prefer self-bookable options over convenience packages unless the price gap is small.

fixtures.summary: Traveler with fixed calendar anchors and a low tolerance for timing uncertainty, who needs every transition and commitment protected from schedule drift.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I have a limited window and a few things I absolutely cannot miss. I do not like arriving late, rearranging at the last minute, or having contingency days eaten by delays.
planning_style_notes: wants buffer built into transition days
needs the plan to stay on track without heroic adjustment

budget_model.splurge_style: pay for timing reliability and direct connections that protect calendar commitments

calendar_anchors.label: Amsterdam concert day 4
calendar_anchors.notes: Fixed calendar commitment. All route planning must protect this date.

mode_anchors.label: Direct or simple connections
mode_anchors.notes: Avoid connection chains with tight transfer windows.

evidence.note: Traveler names fixed date window and a must-protect event on day 4.
Traveler says they dislike last-minute rearrangement and want the plan to stay on track.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: Reliability and schedule integrity are load-bearing for this traveler. The planner should protect fixed commitments as hard anchors before assembling any flexible content.
expected_tensions: Tight date windows reduce room to recover from delays, so transition padding and simple logistics matter more than optimal routing.
planning_implications: Lock calendar anchors first and build all other content around them.
Prefer direct or simple connections over cheaper chains with tight windows.
Flag any transition day that touches a fixed commitment as schedule-sensitive in the plan.

fixtures.summary: Traveler with mobility constraints who needs step-free access, accessible rooms, and logistics that do not assume full ambulatory independence.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I use a wheelchair and need rooms and venues that are genuinely accessible, not just technically compliant on paper. I also need arrivals that do not involve stairs or heroic workarounds.
planning_style_notes: has had bad experiences with nominally accessible rooms that were not actually usable
wants accessibility verified not just listed

lodging_constraints: elevator required if above ground floor

quality_floors.lodging: wheelchair accessible room with verified step-free bathroom

budget_model.splurge_style: pay extra to ensure genuine accessibility rather than nominal compliance

quality_floor_anchors.label: Verified step-free lodging
quality_floor_anchors.notes: Accessibility is a hard requirement, not a preference.

direct_statements: Traveler names mobility constraints explicitly and highlights past failures with nominal accessibility.

confidence_notes: Accessibility requirements are hard constraints not open to budget negotiation.

evidence.note: Traveler declares wheelchair use and step-free requirement as non-negotiable.
Traveler describes past bad experiences with options that were listed as accessible but were not usable.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: Accessibility requirements are hard constraints that must be verified before an option can be considered at all. Budget flexibility exists to guarantee access, not to trade it away.
expected_tensions: Verified accessible options may be fewer, more expensive, or less centrally located, requiring route trade-offs.
planning_implications: Filter all options by verified accessibility data before ranking.
Do not offer nominally accessible options without confirming specifics.
Budget flexibility should go to accessibility assurance first, not comfort upgrades.

fixtures.summary: Family traveler with young children who needs recovery-friendly pacing, stable logistics, and accommodations that work for a group with mixed energy levels.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: We are travelling with two kids under ten. We need flexibility for bad days, rooms that fit a family, and a pace that does not destroy everyone by day four.
planning_style_notes: needs escape hatches when kids hit a wall
wants a mix of engaging activities and low-key base time
does not want to move lodging more than twice

quality_floors.lodging: family room or connecting rooms with space for children

budget_model.splurge_style: spend to protect family sleep quality and avoid logistics that stress children

quality_floor_anchors.label: Family-suitable lodging
quality_floor_anchors.notes: Room must fit the full family. Double rooms shared with children are not acceptable.

tension_flags.description: Family wants to experience multiple areas but children's energy limits daily intensity and move density.

evidence.note: Traveler names children's energy limits and need to avoid pace that exhausts the family.
Traveler sets lodging requirement for family-sized rooms and limits moves to two bases.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: Family pacing and stable logistics dominate this profile. Recovery and friction reduction are protective constraints, not preferences to be balanced against coverage ambitions.
expected_tensions: Desire to show children diverse places competes with the energy and logistics ceiling imposed by travelling with young children.
planning_implications: Build in regular low-demand half-days or base days.
Keep lodging moves to a minimum and prefer longer stays in family-suitable accommodation.
Surface child-friendly activity options alongside adult-oriented alternatives for each day.

fixtures.summary: Frequent traveler who extends business trips with personal days but must stay within policy-like budget and schedule discipline for the whole trip.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I am adding personal days to a business trip. I cannot go over a fixed total budget and my calendar has hard windows. I want the leisure days to feel like real travel, not just filler.
stated_constraints: trip length 10 days total with 4 work days fixed
hard budget ceiling from company policy extended to personal portion
planning_style_notes: comfortable with reliable logistics
does not want to deal with uncertainty on work days
leisure portion should not bleed into work commitments

calendar_anchors.label: Work commitment days 1-4
calendar_anchors.notes: Work days are completely fixed. Leisure must be scheduled around them.

deprioritize: spontaneous detours that could conflict with work schedule

evidence.note: Traveler names fixed work days and a hard budget ceiling from company policy.
Traveler wants leisure days to feel real but cannot let them drift into work windows.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: The work calendar and budget ceiling are absolute constraints. Leisure planning fills the remaining window and should feel substantive, not residual.
expected_tensions: The leisure window is short enough that any plan which overreaches risks bleeding into work commitments or busting the budget.
planning_implications: Design leisure content inside a clearly bounded window, not as additions that compress work time.
Keep all options within policy-level cost discipline even for the personal days.
Surface high-value compact options rather than expansive multi-day plans.

fixtures.summary: Traveler has strict lodging and comfort minimums but also a real budget ceiling that makes those standards costly to maintain everywhere.

raw_inputs.trip_brief: I cannot do bad rooms, chaotic late arrivals, or very poor locations, but I also do not have the budget to do that perfectly in every stop.
planning_style_notes: wants realistic tradeoffs surfaced early
prefers fewer better bases over many weak ones

quality_floors.lodging: quiet private room within practical distance of core sights

quality_floor_anchors.label: Quiet and well-located lodging
quality_floor_anchors.notes: Comfort floor is strong, but budget is not loose.

tension_flags.description: Quality floors and budget ceiling pull against each other, especially in expensive hubs.

evidence.note: Traveler sets firm lodging minimums.
Traveler can accept some convenience tradeoffs, but not full comfort collapse.

intended_interpretation.qualitative_summary: The planner should convert this into route and lodging strategy decisions, not pretend everything can stay high-quality everywhere.
expected_tensions: Quality floors are real, but route breadth or top-season timing may need to contract to preserve them.
planning_implications: Offer fewer-base and shoulder-value scenarios rather than simply cheaper downgraded rooms.
Show quality tradeoffs explicitly when the budget ceiling binds.
